metadata.title: The Obvious Answer (And Why It’s Wrong)

metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The real question is not whether AI will replace software engineers. Accountability rests with the engineer who accepts and merges the pull request. This accountability gap between tool output and human responsibility is widening. Like spreadsheets and accountants in 1985, doing the tasks does not erase the profession.

artifactLines: The real question is not whether AI will replace software engineers.
Accountability rests with the engineer who accepts and merges the pull request.
This accountability gap between tool output and human responsibility is widening.
Like spreadsheets and accountants in 1985, doing the tasks does not erase the profession.

beats.narration_text: The reframing from 'will AI replace engineers' to who accepts the pull request, and the widening Accountability Gap, reshaped how I think about this, especially the 1985 spreadsheet-and-accountants parallel. I want to pressure-test the analogy. Help me: (1) map what accountants actually became after spreadsheets rather than disappearing; (2) define exactly who holds accountability when an AI writes code a human merges; (3) explain why 'a tool can do the tasks' does not equal 'the profession disappears.'
